assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true of the Middle East after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: [Here is the relevant video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/middle-east-20th-century/v/arabia-after-world-war-i) for this question.

In the early 1900s and through WWI, the Arabian Peninsula was nominally under Ottoman rule but was really controlled by various tribal leaders.  Hussein bin Ali, the Sherif of Mecca, was the leading figure in the Hejaz (the Western portion of the Peninsula containing the holy cities of Mecca and Medina) who helped the British against the Ottomans in the Arab Revolt.  He declared himself King of the Hejaz after WWI.

Abdulaziz (known as Ibn Saud) came to power in the Nejd (the central part of the Peninsula including Riyadh in the early 1900s and consolidated his power during WWI (he was not active in the Arab Revolt with the British).  By 1926, he was able to take control of the Hijaz from Hussein bin Ali and declare himself King of Nejd and Hejaz.  This was formally merged into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932.

choices.content: Modern-day Jordan and Iraq emerged from WWI as British Mandates (essentially under British control)
Modern-day Lebanon and Syria emerged from WWI as French Mandates (essentially under French control)
Egyptians won their independence from the British in 1922 through a successful revolt
Kuwait didn't get independence from Britain until 1961
Saudi Arabia was created by Hussein bin Ali after he helped the British against the Ottomans in the Hejaz and Palestine

assessment_items.question_content:
What best described the attitude of the Allies at the Paris Peace Conference after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Woodrow Wilson had already articulated his Fourteen Points in 1918 and came to the Paris Peace Conference in 1919 trying to promote these ideas.  It was idealistic in that, among other things, it called for no secret treaties, free trade and navigation, self-determination, and the League of Nations to arbitrate between nations to avoid war.  

The British and French, however, had suffered far more at the hands of the Germans and were far more interested in blaming Germany for the War and making them pay for it.

This is covered in [this video on the Paris Peace Conference and the Treaty of Versailles](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/paris-peace-conference-and-treaty-of-versailles).
The victorious empires weren't at all interested in decolonization or granting self-determination to groups within their rule.

choices.content: Woodrow Wilson was more idealistic while the British and French were more interested in making Germany pay for the War in resources and territory.
The Allies were eager to work with the Central Powers to promote democracy throughout all of their respective empires.
For fear of causing another war, the Allies worked with the Central Powers to rebuild their respective economies
Britain and France agreed to decolonize their empires but refused to embrace the League of Nations
All of the Allies were eager to exact maximum punishment on the Central Powers

assessment_items.question_content:
How many estimated deaths were caused by World War I (combining military and civilian deaths)?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/deaths-in-world-war-i).

There were roughly 8-10 million military deaths and 6-7 million civilian deaths (it will be impossible to ever have a precise number) so 16 million is a reasonable estimate for total deaths.

choices.content: 2 million
6 million
16 million
60 million
80 million

assessment_items.question_content:
This is the map of Europe in what year?

![Europe Map](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e6927fc01037979abde62bfb60a685b0.gif)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The breakup of the Austro-Hungarian Empire into Czechoslovakia, Austria, Hungary (and parts of other countries) along with the existence of Yugoslavia, Poland and Turkey tell you that this is after World War I (and really after the creation of an independent Turkey from the remains of the Ottoman Empire in 1923).
So that leaves us with 1925 or 1951 as possibilities.

The fact that Germany has not been broken into East and West tells you that this is not after World War II (1951 would have fallen into that category).

Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/paris-peace-conference-and-treaty-of-versailles).

assessment_items.question_content:
Which country was **not** formed from some part of Austria-Hungary after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/paris-peace-conference-and-treaty-of-versailles).

Austria, Hungary and Czechoslovakia came directly from Austria-Hungary.  

Parts of Yugoslavia (Slovenia, Croatia and Bosnia) came from Austria-Hungary.

Lithuania did emerge as a new country after the War, but all of its territory came from the former Russian Empire.

choices.content: Austria
Hungary
Czechoslovakia
Yugoslavia
Lithuania

assessment_items.question_content:
The Treaty of Versailles between the Allies and Germany at the end of WWI stipulated all of the following **except**:

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This is covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/more-detail-on-the-treaty-of-versailles-and-germany).

The Treaty of Versailles did not call for France to occupy the coal and steel-rich Ruhr region of Germany.  France did eventually do this in 1923 after Germany was unable to continue reparation payments (which added to German humiliation and led more to support extreme political parties like the National Socialists (Nazis)).

choices.content: Payment of reparations in the form of currency and actual resources
French occupation of the Ruhr region of Germany
Demilitarization of all of Germany west of the Rhine and 50km east of it
Shipping coal produced in Germany's Saar region to France
Forbidding German unification with Austria
Limiting German armed forces to 100,000 troops
Assigning "war guilt" to Germany

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of these events or facts provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/paris-peace-conference-and-treaty-of-versailles).

It is completely debatable whether Germany should have been assigned full "war guilt" as the Treaty of Versailles later did.  One could argue that the Austrians were overly aggressive in their ultimatum to the Serbians (and their later declaration of War).  The Russians also began mobilizing in defense of Serbia before Germany had declared war.
The Zimmerman Telegram helped instigate American entry into the War in 1917 but did not trigger the war itself.

Bosnia was annexed by the Austrians.  One could argue that this helped precipitate the assassination of Franz Ferdinand and thus helped trigger the war.  The Germans, however, had nothing to do with this.
The Anglo-German arms race (both on sea and land) helped make the environment ripe for an all-out war between these two powers.  However, the British were as complicit in this as the Germans.

The existence of the Schlieffen Plan by itself did not lead to the war. Germany quickly declared war on Russian and France only days after the Austro-Hungarians declared war on Serbia.  They used Russian mobilization as a pretext, but one could argue that they needlessly escalated what would have been a regional conflict in the Balkans to a pan-European (and eventually much of the world) conflict involving the major powers.  The speed with which they were able to roll into Belgium indicates that they were merely looking for a trigger to execute on their Schlieffen Plan.  

So, the speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war provides the best justification for blaming Germany for World War I.

choices.content: the Zimmerman Telegram
the annexation of Bosnia
the Anglo-German naval race
the existence of the Schlieffen Plan prior to the War
the speed and aggression with which the Germans engaged the French and Russians shortly after Austria's declaration of war

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following was *not* a theme of Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Covered in [this video](/humanities/history/euro-hist/ww1-aftermath/v/woodrow-wilson-s-fourteen-points).

The Fourteen Points make no mention of redistributing Germany's colonies and, if anything, suggested a move away from empires altogether.

Woodrow Wilson's fifth (of the fourteen) point was:

*5. A free, open-minded, and absolutely impartial adjustment of all colonial claims, based upon a strict observance of the principle that in determining all such questions of sovereignty the interests of the populations concerned must have equal weight with the equitable claims of the government whose title is to be determined*

This idea was not in-line with having empires at all (empires usually involve control of a region irrespective of the desires of the population).

choices.content: Open treaties (no secret covenants)
Free trade between nations
The idea that people should have a say in who governs them (self determination)
Formation of the League of Nations
Calling for German colonies to be made part of the French and British empires
